item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sheer surrealness of the piece and horror that's derived from it are what really sold this tale for me. Besides, the subject matter is indeed pretty scary. +1.

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your insight here. This is still one of my favorite tales and I come back to it every so often to re-read it. I hope many more enjoy it!

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded:
Certainly. Whenever you can get inspiration take it and jot the idea down. Even if it comes from a not-so-normal source. It's a good way to do it and as said before it works well. If it works don't fix it I say. I wouldn't mind seeing more of this idea as well, it's interesting and got me to read through the entire thing.

I'll actually read those other tales too later.

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded:
A little concerning, but it certainly seems to work…

Take it where you can get it, right?

Most of my story ideas begin as an image of a person doing an unusual thing (tending a garden of skeletons, fighting giant tentacles in a stairwell, opening at the chest to use ribs as spears).

"Compassionate brain theft" is a strong one, and all the follow-up questions (why would this thing be happening? What kind of person would have that reaction to it?) had interesting answers. I still intend to write more about Mo eventually, and follow her blind devotion to whatever its terrible conclusion might be.

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded:
She squirmed and cried when I changed her and the gentle, soothing things I whispered as I put her to bed combined with the image of a doctor cutting someone's brain out to make Mo. Everything else followed from that.

That's… Certainly a way of getting inspiration for something I guess. A little concerning, but it certainly seems to work…

I don't think this is the best thing I've written for the site, but 10-some articles later it's still my favorite. Grand prize for Doomcon is an featured spot, so I'm choosing this article. To celebrate, here's three fun facts:

+ three facts
– hide block
  • The inspiration for this came after I re-read SCP-2003 right before taking care of my then-newborn baby. She squirmed and cried when I changed her and the gentle, soothing things I whispered as I put her to bed combined with the image of a doctor cutting someone's brain out to make Mo. Everything else followed from that.
  • The section where the reality bender destroys everyone was composed over the course of about three weeks during my daily drive to work. Some of my favorite writers here have said that their process is like improvisational jazz and they write as they go. Mine is not. The whole thing was painstakingly visualized then put into words bit by bit over what I would conservatively estimate to be about 20 hours. When I finally wrote it down, I had it pretty much memorized and typed it out all at once with very few changes.
  • Evelyn gets away with being so snarky because she's unnaturally gifted at sticking machines into people's brains and making them do things, which makes her valuable. However, she's also a mole for the Chaos Insurgency, and her collusion with them was actually what allowed them to sabotage her Site and cause the fateful power outage at the end of this. I wanted to write a tale showing all that, and maybe still will someday.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing I have ever read on this site has quite disturbed me this much.

Monstrous

Thank you! This is, in all seriousness, the highest praise I could imagine for my writing.

There's something horrifying to me about the way that your brain and your self are inescapably connected, and involuntary surgery is always scary, as is the idea of being used as an actual cog in a machine. The part that was the most chilling to write about was Mo's genuine compassion and belief that what she's doing is right and necessary (and the thought that she might be correct).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good god. Nothing I have ever read on this site has quite disturbed me this much. I have a deep and abiding disgust/fear of lobotomies, psychosurgery and loss of bodily autonomy. This story managed to hit me on all counts and I congratulate you on it. It honestly reminds me of the part of District Nine where they are discussing vivisecting the main character while he sits there listening to them, and it provoked the same response from me.

Monstrous, but well written. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good storytelling arises by establishing the usual, then describing some unusual event. To do this in a short story within the Foundation universe, where everything is already unusual, is solid writing!

I was helicoptered in to the middle of Mo's life, grew to like her (a must for any main character), then appreciate the reality she had to deal with - and the consequences for her and her "patients".

It's still reasonably new, so I hope you enjoy a lot more than 42 upvotes, so I add my +1 now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: now this is the fuckin shit about reality benders that i have been waiting for

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll definitely keep reading your stuff.

A downvote here doesn't mean I think you're a bad author, or anything. I just didn't like this one in particular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I say pulpy, I'm not being entirely metaphorical, either: I love writing that pulls you in with unconventional sensory detail like texture or sound, and this was definitely a very chunk-laden tale in all the appropriately gory ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for commenting -

+ that's just, like, your opinion, man
- JK I really appreciate it

I can get the dissonance being off-putting. Part of it comes from originally wanting this to be more of a character study and ending up at straightforward sci-fi/horror. Part of it is style choices I'm still getting the hang of — even in the context of detraction I'm still proud to see "dream-like" used to describe something I've written, and I gave little description of the characters and environments to keep them sketchy, so the reader can fill them in. But in hindsight it wouldn't have hurt to give some kind of visual signifier to everybody, like mentioning Evelyn wears glasses or describing her appearance as "sharp-edged."

I definitely want the story to be more than just the surface, but the challenge for me is doing that quickly; I've been writing on the assumption that other people read the site like I do, a few minutes at a time throughout the day, and trying to make stuff that can be delivered in that framework. I got some feedback on this one that it would have been OK to make it a little longer, and in the future I plan to try taking things a bit slower so the characters have more time to develop and the reader has more time to enjoy the ride.

There are some plot points that don't really stand up to intense scrutiny — Ken venting to Mo the way he does, the power going out in the middle of their surgery — but those are things I tend to let slide for the sake of getting to the cool stuff without being bogged down. I'm an adamant defender of handwaving for the sake of entertainment, and just hope that in those cases the content is interesting enough that the reader lets me get away with it.

I'm bummed that you didn't enjoy the read, but even so, I still sort of like seeing the characters described as vague. I was going for a trippy, dreamlike experience, and feel like I nailed it, so I'm glad I had the opportunity to hear from somebody it didn't work for. Some of that missing 25% will probably fill in as I experiment/relearn a little (I wrote Ethics a really long time ago, so not counting SCP-3661 this is actually the first piece of creative writing I've finished in almost 2 years), so I hope you'll keep checking out the stuff that comes next to see how it develops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's really flattering. I would never have thought to describe my writing as pulpy, but it is and I love it. By far the greatest compliment, though, is saying that I made this look easy. Thanks for reading it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I hesitated to make this post until I could articulate my feelings on it.

I feel the almost dream-like nature of the tale detracts heavily from the subject matter.

If Mo is supposed to be one of the test subjects, this works, though I'm still not a huge fan.

If Mo is not supposed to be one of the test subjects, the lack of solid description kinda pulls me out. I never really got engrossed in the story, it was all on the surface.

Beyond that, I felt some of the interactions felt implausible, and overall just didn't work for me.

What's frustrating, is this feels like 75% of something great, and there's just 25% missing. I never "see" where any of the characters are. They're just conversations, with some vague implications.

Thematically, it works, but it's definitely a frustrating read, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, what a read!

*The* paragraph, *the* paragraph that the entire story builds toward, *the* paragraph where everything goes to hell…just stunning writing. Short bursts of chaos, one after another, but filled with beauty throughout.

A hell of a piece, this, and I can't wait to see, as you say, "Mo".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, that's really nice.

The SRA going down is admittedly pretty contrived - they'd have to be pretty arrogant or short-sighted to not make that more of a priority. I justified it to myself by thinking that the anchor probably needs a lot more than just electricity, and can't be powered by a backup. Or, maybe, there was some kind of sabotage or major breach happening that we'll find out about in another story. Really though I just needed it turned off and was willing to resort to handwaving for the sake of pace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so fucked up and I love it. Sorry I didn't get a chance to give feedback before this was posted but I very much enjoyed reading this final product. Such visceral imagery. The site needs more stuff like this. Makes Humes and SRA's less of an abstract thinkpiece and more of something you can touch and feel and bleed. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! Looking for the event horizon where people give up their humanity in order to protect their existence is my favorite theme on the site. Mo does these terrible things because she's a good person, not in spite of it, which is what makes her intriguing to me. I haven't been able to prove to myself that she's wrong about any of it.

Looking back I saw how many stages of this you helped with, so thanks again for that. Looking forward to pushing deeper and deeper into whatever the heart of this thing turns out to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Keter cakes are 871, not 837. c.c

I don't get the significance of the final line, but it's overall well written and interesting. :) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Evelyn turned, started to say something, trailed off when she saw. The head in cell B-2 had woken up and started screaming.

In cell C-2, the head kept screaming.

is it a mistake?

It's a terrifying tale either way. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God I love this. Couldn't upvote harder.

Also, very nice fakeouts during the test, and the description of reality collapsing was amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concise, punchy and pulpy, with a great writing style that knows its strengths and pulls them off with ease and verve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gorgeous prose, doesn't drag on longer than it needs to, hits hard and simple. Here's rooting for you - I'm looking very forward to anything else you might come up with.

edit: the SRA running out of battery is kind of hackneyed, ill admit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this tale. Fantastic contrast of humanity and inhumanity, with great action and emotional weight. Hearty +1

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded:
What would you do to save the world?

+ So much credit is due
– hide block

I had tons of help with this from pretty much the entire site. Everyone below is partially responsible for these atrocities:

Zyn for encouragement at the idea and brainstorming stage.

Modern_Erasmus, psul, Zyn, and everyone else on the message board about Scranton Reality Anchor - all the ideas about that helped tie together the atmosphere.

Scorpion451, psul, and Petrograd helped out with great insight into the horrifyingly real world of brain-in-a-jar experiments.

Modern_Erasmus, DrChandra, and Kakroom for checking it out as a draft, I truly appreciate that.

Everyone in chat who indulged me in bullshitting about Hume levels. Jarvis for generating most of the names, keep up the good work J-dog.

Credit is also due to the author of SCP-2003; this all started when I read that a couple weeks ago and got hit with this mental picture of a kindhearted doctor saying soothing things into someone's ear as she cuts their brain out.

And to the author of the keter cakes, I hope it's okay that I used your thing here. It's a classic.

Also, thanks to everyone who read and/or commented on this so far and everyone who does going forward. I would never have written this if you weren't here to read it.

Even after all this, the following pun is the most disgusting thing I've ever written:

There's so much Mo to come.
